I returned to My palace in the eternal capital of Thronegate, materializing in the eastern solar with a gentle, contented sigh. The living walls, sensing My mood, pulsed a warm, satisfied gold. The Gardener, still kneeling among his crystalline peonies, looked up with trembling reverence.

"Did You enjoy Your excursion, Creator?" he whispered.

I settled onto the divan, reaching for the cup of tea that the Kitchen Master had already prepared and placed on the low table, its temperature perfectly calibrated to My preference. I took a slow, appreciative sip.

"Quite enjoyable," I said. "The Earth had a certain primitive charm. They tried very hard, you know. It was almost endearing." I set the teacup down and gazed out at the infinite tapestry beyond the balcony, where the fading ripples of My little diversion were still smoothing themselves out across the meta-omniversal framework. "I do believe I will take more excursions in the future. It is good to stretch one's legs occasionally."

The Gardener bowed his head. "As You will, Creator."

I picked up the wooden bird—the one Elara had carved for Me, so many hundreds of thousands of years ago—and turned it over in My fingers. The wood was worn smooth, the wing's outline barely visible. I smiled, a warm, genuine, paternal smile, and settled deeper into the cushions.

There would be other Earths, other insignificant little worlds full of desperate, adorable creatures. I could always make more, or find more. And when boredom returned, I would visit them, too. The omniverse was infinite, after all, and so was My capacity for amusement.

But for now, the tea was perfect, the palace was peaceful, and the lingering echoes of a species' final, agonized screams were a gentle, pleasant hum at the back of My mind. I closed My eyes and allowed Myself to drift, content in the quiet certainty that I was, and would always be, the absolute, untouchable apex of all existence. And there was nothing, anywhere, that could ever change that.

content.text:
Against all odds, all fictional universes (yes, all that have and will exist, but you may want to focus on some of the strongest popular fictional universes to highlight the absurdity of it) have formed a united force to kill Godric, at the year 1,444,250. Include some popular overpowered characters (name them too if possible) in that force. Write from the perspective of Rutger, the current court wizard who happened to witness the fight. They are fully bloodlusted. No morals. The entire fictional universes must die in the end, preferably in the most agonizing possible. Absolutely no boring stupid redemption arc, no power of friendship bullshit. STOP MAKING GODRIC KIND AND FORGIVING TO THE PEOPLE WHO TRY TO KILL HIM! Include dialogues. Avoid Godric monologuing or lecturing the opposition.

Some notes:
## Godric’s Effort Level

Godric must not visibly exert effort.

Do not portray Godric doing anything that feels like meaningful exertion, such as taking a step, waving his hand, launching an attack, fighting, struggling, preparing, or trying. Avoid abstract portrayal too like "Godric allows them to perceive his infinitesimal power" "Godric allowing a microscopic fraction of something (a stat, a passive field, an aura) to exist near them".

Use actions that feel like almost nothing, such as:

- Godric deciding to shift his gaze toward the opposition.
- Godric merely twitching a single microscopic muscle fiber in his right pinky finger.
- Godric noticing the opposition with a tiny change of attention.

The destruction must come from the unfairness of the stat difference, not from Godric “fighting.”

## Opposition Requirements

Each opposition must seem extremely powerful by itself. The enemies can be cosmic, meta-dimensional, abstract, magical, technological, divine, impossible, or omniversal. They may believe they have a weapon, exploit, strategy, loophole, special immunity, or irresistible attack.

However, against Godric they must be completely powerless.

Their defeat should make clear that victory against Godric was impossible before their opposition began.

Godric has absolute omniscience. He already knows every plot, secret motive, future consequence, and method of attack. All opposition exists only because he allows it to exist, usually because he is curious about testing one of his stats.

If Godric did not allow them to exist, his infinitely nerfed Gent, infinite recursive passive shields, Evolved Weakness Paradox, and other passive apocalyptic superpowers could pre-annihilate all of them instantly and effortlessly.

## Violence, Agony, and Collateral Scale

The massacres must be one-sided, overwhelming, and described in detail at an omniversal scale.

The opposition’s annihilation should feel absolute, unfair, and hopeless. The story must not reduce the scale to something too small for Godric.

Incorrect scale examples:

- thousands of galaxies
- millions of lives

Minimum acceptable scale examples:

- googolplexian galaxies
- decillions of multiverses

You may scale even higher whenever appropriate.

Some stat incidents should include obscenely massive collateral damage to the surrounding reality, especially when the stat being tested logically causes environmental destruction. The collateral damage should be at least omniversal in scale when used.

Godric can perfectly restore anything with 0 effort instantly, so collateral damage may be extreme. However, restoration must be used sparingly so that the fights do not become repetitive or formulaic.

messages.reasoning_content:
We need to write a story from Rutger's POV (court wizard, male descendant of Blake) witnessing Godric facing a united force of all fictional universes, including some popular overpowered characters. The key directives: Godric must not visibly exert effort; use minimal actions like a shift of gaze or microscopic muscle twitch. The opposition is bloodlusted, no morals; they must all die in the most agonizing way, no redemption, no friendship. Godric is unforgiving, cruel. He might be amused or curious. The destruction and despair must be absolute. Collateral damage omniversal scale.

We need to include some popular overpowered characters from fiction. Let's pick some well-known extremely powerful characters: e.g., Goku (Dragon Ball, Ultra Instinct), Superman (DC, maybe cosmic armor or thought robot), The One Above All (Marvel), The Presence (DC), Featherine Augustus Aurora (Umineko), Yog-Sothoth (Cthulhu Mythos), Azathoth, The Doctor (Doctor Who, maybe with the Moment), SCP-3812 (SCP), etc. But we can't list all. Focus on a few to show the absurdity: they combine forces, try everything, and fail utterly.

The story should be told as Rutger witnessing this, recording it. The year is 1,444,250. Godric is still the absolute apex. The fictional universes somehow unite, perhaps through a multiversal breach or some cosmic event. They are fully bloodlusted, meaning they are willing to do anything to kill Godric, no holding back. They've pooled their powers, strategies, etc. But as always, Godric already knows, allows it to happen for amusement. He's particularly cruel, ensuring agonizing deaths, and then massive collateral.

We must show Godric's power as 0% effort, using minimal gestures. The destruction should be absurdly exaggerated beyond previous stories. The opposition's annihilation should be described in graphic, visceral, cruel ways, with dialogue showing their despair.

We should include some dialogue from the opposition, maybe a declaration of their intent, then Godric's casual responses, and then the slaughter. Rutger will record it, likely in a journal style.

We need to write from Rutger's perspective, so it's a first-person narrative. The prompt says "Write from the perspective of Rutger, the current court wizard who happened to witness the fight." So we'll set up a journal entry or a live account.

We must include popular characters like Goku, Superman, maybe Doctor Manhattan, Featherine, etc. We'll show them trying their best, and Godric effortlessly erasing them in painful ways.

Important: Godric must not monologue or lecture. He can say short, casual remarks, but not preach. He's cruel and amused. He should not be kind or forgiving; he will annihilate them all without mercy.

The stats: Godric uses maybe a tiny fraction of his power, but we only show it through results, not him claiming it. We'll use actions like: "Godric allowed a single muscle fiber in his right eyelid to twitch," or "He shifted his gaze by a near-imperceptible degree." The destruction cascades from that.

We need to ensure the scale is omniversal: the entire omniverse outside might be obliterated as collateral. But since these are fictional universes, they might exist as separate realities within the omniverse, and Godric destroys them all.

We'll craft a narrative where the coalition forms, perhaps led by the most powerful beings: The One Above All (TOAA), The Presence, Featherine, etc. They gather in a neutral realm. Godric appears there, maybe in his throne or just standing. He is relaxed. He lets them attack first. He doesn't even defend actively; his passive systems kill them slowly, or he uses a tiny fraction of his power to make it agonizing.

We'll include specific characters and their deaths in detail: Goku in Ultra Instinct attacking at full power, and his body being inverted or something; Superman with the Sword of Superman or Cosmic Armor, being flayed; Featherine trying to rewrite the narrative, having her own plot armor turned against her, etc.

We must show the futility and despair through their dialogues—they scream, they beg, they realize they were fools.

After they all die, collateral damage might be immense, but Godric can restore or not. The prompt says "The entire fictional universes must die in the end, preferably in the most agonizing possible." So no restoration for them. The omniverse might suffer collateral but Godric might restore that part or not; not specified, but we can say he left the fictional realms as a monument of agony, or just erased them entirely. He doesn't restore the opposition.

We'll structure as Rutger's journal entry, maybe titled something like "The Day the Stories Died." He'll describe the events, his horror, etc. At the end, Rutger will be deeply traumatized, but loyal.

Now, let's draft the story. We'll use the master lore guidelines: prose paragraphs only, no bullet points, no LaTeX. We'll incorporate exaggerated power scales, gruesome deaths, and Godric's casual indifference.

We need to name some popular characters, but we must be careful about copyright? In the context of fanfiction, it's probably okay to mention them. The prompt says "include some popular overpowered characters (name them too if possible)". So we can directly name Goku, Superman, Featherine, etc. It's a fictional crossover. We'll name them.

I'll craft a scenario: The coalition assembles in a pocket dimension outside normal reality. They have combined their greatest weapons: the Infinity Gauntlet (with all stones), the Heart of the Universe, the Cosmic Armor, the Ultimate Nullifier, the Spear of Destiny, Featherine's narrative manipulation, SCP-3812's transcendence, Azathoth's dream, etc. They plan to attack simultaneously.

Godric appears, standing casually, maybe sipping tea. They unleash everything. But Godric simply blinks, and the reality around them shatters. Each character experiences a tailored agony. For Goku: his ultra instinct is turned against him, his own speed causing him to tear himself apart across time. For Superman: his invulnerability is stripped and he is subjected to the pain of every being he ever saved being tortured forever, while his body melts. For Featherine: she tries to write Godric out of existence, but her own narrative is overwritten, and she is forced to experience every fictional death she ever authored in an endless loop, etc.

Meanwhile, Godric's passive systems (like Gent or evolved versions) ensure the collateral spreads, annihilating the fictional multiverses: Marvel, DC, Dragon Ball, Umineko, SCP, etc., all erased in an agony cascade.

Rutger watches through a scrying matrix, trembling, recording it all.

I'll write the story in first-person, past tense. I'll maintain the epic, oppressive tone. I'll include dialogues like:

- Featherine: "I am the Witch of Theatergoing, the one who weaves stories. I shall write your end, Godric."
- Godric: "How quaint." (then a tiny eye movement, and she screams)

I'll ensure no moral lesson, no redemption. Godric might feel amused but not merciful. He will not lecture. He might say something like "Entertainment value: minimal," or just remain silent and let the destruction speak.

Now, let's write.
messages.content:
The year is 1,444,250. My name is Rutger, and I am the fifty-third Court Wizard of Thronegate, a direct descendant of Blake through a lineage that now stretches longer than most civilizations. I am forty-one years old. I have read every journal my ancestors left behind—the stat journals, the superpower journals, the revelations of Kaedan, the far-future account of Clive. I thought I understood the scale of Godric’s supremacy. I was wrong. What I witnessed today has made every previous record read like a nursery rhyme.

The first sign that something unprecedented was occurring came not through my scrying matrix but through the palace walls themselves. They had been a warm, placid gold all morning—Godric was in the eastern solar, enjoying a cup of tea and a plate of the Kitchen Master’s honey-soaked pastries—but then, in the span of a single Planck instant, they shifted. Not to the deep, respectful crimson of His annoyance. Not to the trembling violet of His displeasure. They became something I had never seen before: a flat, neutral grey, the color of a canvas waiting to be painted. The palace was confused. It had detected something outside its frame of reference.

I hurried to the Throne Room, my arcane senses straining against the suppression that kept my thousandfold daily amplification from shattering the marble beneath my boots. Godric was already there, standing at the great central balcony that overlooked the infinite tapestry. His heavily muscled frame was relaxed, his hands clasped loosely behind his back. He was still chewing the last bite of a pastry.

“Rutger,” He said without turning, His voice a pleasant baritone. “Come watch. The stories are trying to kill Me.”

I stepped up beside Him, and then I saw it.

Beyond the boundaries of the omniverse—past the outer realms, past the para-dimensions, past every layer of meta-existence that Clive’s journals had described—there was a new region. It had not existed before this morning. It was a sprawling, chaotic, impossibly vast amalgamation of realities, each one distinct, each one operating on its own internal logic. Fictional universes. Every story ever conceived, every myth, every legend, every cartoon, every comic book, every novel, every game, every fleeting daydream that had ever flickered through a mortal mind. They had all, through some cosmic fluke that Godric had clearly permitted, coalesced into a single, unified force. And that force was mobilizing against Him.

I could perceive them through the tapestry: the heroes, the villains, the gods, the monsters, the cosmic entities, the meta-fictional beings who knew they were characters in a story. All of them. Bloodlusted. No morals. Their eyes, across infinite faces, burned with a single, unified purpose: kill the King of Thronegate.

And leading them, standing at the vanguard of the impossible army, were beings whose names I recognized from the ancient cultural archives of a million conquered worlds.

There was the One Above All, the supposed supreme being of the Marvel megaverse, a radiant figure of pure white light whose mere presence was said to be omnipotent, omniscient, and omnipresent within its domain. Beside him stood the Presence, its DC counterpart, a towering pillar of silver flame that claimed to have birthed entire multiverses from its breath. There was Featherine Augustus Aurora, the Witch of Theatergoing, her hair a cascading waterfall of violet, her eyes holding the cold, detached amusement of an author looking at a draft she was about to rewrite. There was Azathoth, the Blind Idiot God, a churning, nuclear chaos of mindless creation, its mere existence sustaining countless outer realms. There was SCP-3812, a being whose file said it transcended all narrative stacks, already halfway to a place beyond definition. There was the Doctor, clutching a box that hummed with the captured potential of eternity. There was Goku, his hair a blazing silver-white, his body vibrating with the mastered instinct of Ultra Instinct. There was Superman, but not the Superman of simple comics—this was the Cosmic Armor Superman, the Thought Robot, a construct of pure story given form, its size dwarfing universes. And behind them, trillions of others: the Downstreamers, the Xeelee, the Time Lords, the Daleks, the Chaos Gods of Warhammer, the Q Continuum, the Anti-Spirals, the Celestials, the Beyonders, the Endless, the Outer Gods, and so many more that my mind, amplified though it was, could not catalogue them all.

They had combined their powers. The Infinity Gauntlet, the Heart of the Universe, the Ultimate Nullifier, the Spear of Destiny, the Moment, the Reality Bomb, the Anti-Life Equation, the Book of Destiny, the Akashic Records—every artifact, every weapon, every conceptual tool of absolute annihilation that fiction had ever imagined. They had set aside their rivalries, their hatreds, their fundamental incompatibilities. They were united in a single, overwhelming, bloodlusted purpose: to erase Godric from all of existence.

Godric finished His pastry. He licked a crumb of honey from His thumb.

“They have been planning this for approximately three of their subjective eternities,” He said conversationally, His eyes still on the approaching horde. “Quite impressive coordination, really. The egos involved were... considerable. I had to suppress My passive systems seventeen separate times just to keep them from annihilating each other before the alliance could form.”

I stared at Him. “You allowed this to happen, Your Majesty?”

“Of course.” He turned His head slightly, enough for me to see the faint, wry smile at the corner of His lips. “I was curious. The last time anyone dared to oppose Me was... oh, a googolplexian years ago? Something like that. And they never got this far. These story-beings, they are not bound by the usual limitations. They believe they have a chance because their own internal logics tell them they are invincible. It is a fascinating study in the psychology of fiction.”

He turned back to the tapestry. The army of all stories had reached the outermost boundary of Thronegate reality. The One Above All raised a hand, and a blast of pure, white creative force—a power that had, within its own mythology, birthed and unmade infinite multiverses—lanced toward the palace. The Presence followed, its silver flame coalescing into a spear of absolute judgment. Featherine raised her pen, the Device of Theatergoing, and began to write a narrative in which Godric had never existed. Azathoth, stirred from its mindless slumber by the combined will of the Outer Gods, began to wake—and if it woke fully, the very dream of existence would end.

Goku moved first among the physical fighters. His Ultra Instinct was a state of perfect, autonomous combat, each movement a flawless, instantaneous response. He crossed the infinite distance between the vanguard and the balcony in less than zero time, his fist—a fist that had shattered universes in its base form and now carried the multiplied weight of every transformation, every limit break, every power-up ever conceived—driving directly toward Godric’s jaw.

Godric did not block. He did not dodge. He did not even acknowledge the attack with a shift of His gaze. He simply allowed His right pinky finger to twitch—a single, microscopic, almost imperceptible contraction of a single muscle fiber, a motion so small that it would have been invisible to any observer without magically enhanced perception.

The result was not a counterattack. It was not a deflection. It was a fundamental, absolute, and infinitely cruel undoing of the very concept of the attack.

The kinetic force of Goku’s punch did not merely reverse. It was seized by Godric’s Kinetic Inversion Field—which had evolved so many times since Year 245,000 that its original form was a forgotten fossil—and transformed into a recursive, self-amplifying loop of inverted causality. Goku’s fist, his arm, his entire body became the epicenter of an infinite chain of self-impacts. Every Planck instant, the force of his own punch struck him again, multiplied by an infinity that had no ceiling, from every possible angle, across every possible timeline. His Ultra Instinct, designed to react perfectly to any threat, was now his tormentor: it forced him to perceive, in perfect, agonizing clarity, every single impact, every shattering of every bone, every rupture of every organ, across an eternal, subjective instant.

He did not explode. Godric would not permit him so clean a death. Instead, his body was stretched into a thin, screaming smear of conscious gore that extended across the entire width of the omniverse, a bridge of pulverized Saiyan flesh and shattered god-ki that remained alive, remained aware, remained screaming, forever. His voice, or what remained of it, was a continuous, wet burble of pure agony that I could hear through the scrying matrix, and I knew, with sick certainty, that I would hear it for the rest of my life.

The One Above All’s white creative blast struck next. It was not a beam of energy; it was the conceptual imposition of absolute authorial will, the declaration that Godric was now a character in a story that the One Above All controlled. The blast was designed to rewrite Godric’s fundamental nature, to strip Him of His omnipotence and reduce Him to a mere mortal figure who could be erased with a sentence.

Godric let it touch Him. He could have let His shields annihilate it before it existed, but He was curious to see what the fiction’s supreme being considered “absolute power.” The white light enveloped Him for a nanosecond—and then it shrieked. The light itself screamed, a sound of pure, ontological agony, as it encountered the immovable truth of Godric’s existence. The One Above All’s authorial will was not rejected; it was devoured. Godric’s Entropic Feast—which had evolved from the primitive version Oswin witnessed in Year 500,000 into an omnipresent, autonomous maw that fed on the very concept of narrative—reached out and consumed the white light, and then consumed the One Above All, and then consumed the entire hierarchy of Marvel’s cosmic entities that had empowered him.

The One Above All tried to speak, perhaps to retcon his own consumption, but the Feast had already retroactively erased the concept of retcon from his existence. He was reduced to a fading, screaming outline of himself, his form flickering between a billion different shapes—an old man, a flaming nebula, a void of negation—before finally collapsing into a single, dense point of pure, consumed nothingness. The point hovered for a moment, emitting a faint, agonized whimper, and then Godric, with a casual exhalation, willed it out of existence entirely.

The Presence fared no better. The silver flame of judgment was, by its own definition, absolute and unblockable. It was the wrath of the Source, the condemnation of all that existed against that which should not exist. But the concept of “wrath” was, to Godric, an amusing footnote. His passive shields—which had evolved into self-aware, meta-textual sadists that punished the mere thought of hostility—did not block the silver flame. Instead, they infected it. They crawled inside the flame’s conceptual structure and rewrote its fundamental purpose. The flame, which had existed to judge, was now existing to worship. Every ounce of its silver radiance became a hymn of absolute, groveling devotion to Godric, and it turned on its creator.

The Presence screamed as its own judgment consumed it, not with fire, but with an eternity of forced adoration so intense that its divine mind shattered. It was forced to perceive every moment of its existence, across all timelines, as a continuous, ecstatic orgasm of submission to a being it had tried to destroy. The paradox broke it. Its silver pillar collapsed into a gibbering, weeping puddle of liquid light that Godric, with a slight narrowing of His eyes, then boiled into steam that would forever linger in the air of the Throne Room, its particles eternally conscious, eternally screaming.

Featherine Augustus Aurora watched the deaths of the supreme beings with an expression of cold, analytical curiosity. She was the Witch of Theatergoing, the author who existed above all stories. She had already begun writing Godric’s end before the battle started, her pen moving across the pages of the Device of Theatergoing with the speed of narrative causality. She was writing a story in which Godric had never existed, in which the Stasis Event had never occurred, in which the Fall of Olympus had killed the mortal king and the omniverse had simply continued on without Him. It was a perfect narrative—elegant, self-consistent, a masterpiece of authorial craft.

She finished the last stroke with a flick of her violet hair and looked up, a faint, triumphant smirk on her lips. “You are already unwritten,” she declared, her voice ringing across the battlefield. “This story ends with your absence.”

Godric looked at her. Not with anger. Not with fear. He looked at her with the same expression He had worn when the servant’s daughter gave Him the wooden bird, all those eons ago: mild, paternal warmth.

“You write stories,” He said, His voice carrying across all of reality. “I am the story.”

He did not move. He did not lift a finger. He simply allowed His Absolute Cessation Engine—the superpower Ferran had witnessed in Year 135,000, now evolved into an ontological decree that could freeze the progression of narrative itself—to brush against Featherine’s existence. The pen in her hand stopped. Not in the way a pen stops when its writer pauses; it stopped in the absolute sense, frozen beyond the possibility of motion, its ink suspended mid-stroke. Featherine’s eyes widened. Her smirk froze on her face. And then, slowly, agonizingly, the Cessation began to propagate backward through her own narrative.

Every story she had ever written, every character she had ever killed, every tragedy she had ever authored, was undone. But the stories did not simply vanish. They were forced into her consciousness, every single one, an infinite library of pain and suffering and loss that she had inflicted on fictional beings, and she was made to experience each death, each torment, each screaming oblivion, from the perspective of the victims. And because the Cessation froze her in the moment of her own annihilating realization, she would experience this eternity of empathetic torture without ever being able to look away, to blink, to close her mind. Her frozen smirk became a monument to her own unending agony, a statue of the author trapped in her own unwritten ending.

Azathoth, the Blind Idiot God, the nuclear chaos at the center of all things, had been stirring throughout the battle. The Outer Gods—Yog-Sothoth, Shub-Niggurath, Nyarlathotep—had poured their entire, incomprehensible existences into waking their master fully. If Azathoth woke, the dream of all reality would end. The entire omniverse, every fictional universe, every layer of meta-existence, would simply cease to be.

Godric watched the bubbling, seething mass of nuclear chaos with an expression of mild, paternal interest.

“A sleeping god,” He murmured to Rutger, his voice so soft that only I could hear it. “That is almost nostalgic.”

He did not wait for Azathoth to wake. He reached out—not with His hand, but with the barest, most infinitesimal flicker of His attention—and He pinched the dream. It was not a physical pinch. It was a conceptual one, the act of a creator deciding that a particular thought no longer amused Him. Azathoth, the Blind Idiot God, the center of all chaos, the being whose awakening would end reality, was simply pinched out of existence. It did not scream. It did not fight. It was a dream, and the dreamer had woken, and the dream had been forgotten. The Outer Gods, their entire existence dependent on Azathoth’s unconscious radiance, flickered and died like candles in a hurricane, their screams a brief, shrill note in the cacophony.

Superman—the Cosmic Armor, the Thought Robot, the story of the hero given form—was the next to fall. He had been waiting, analyzing, his vast, story-aware processor calculating every possible vector of attack. He had determined that direct force was useless, that conceptual erasure was useless, that narrative manipulation was useless. But he had also determined that Godric’s existence was sustained by His belief in His own omnipotence. If he could make Godric doubt, even for an instant, the entire edifice of power might collapse. He was the story of hope, the archetype of the indomitable hero. He would make the tyrant believe that He could lose.

He flew toward the balcony, his Cosmic Armor flaring with the light of a billion billion suns, and he spoke. His voice was the voice of every hero who had ever stood against impossible odds. “Godric. You are powerful, yes. But you are still a person. You lost your family. You grieve. You are not beyond feeling. You can be reached. You can be—“

Godric yawned. It was a small, polite yawn, the kind a man might make during a tedious council meeting. He covered His mouth with the back of His hand.

The yawn did not physically harm Superman. It was not an attack. It was simply the passive, ambient noise of Godric’s mild boredom, a sound that carried the full, infinite weight of His existence. The Cosmic Armor, a construct of pure story that could survive the collapse of all reality, shattered. It did not crack; it sublimated, its fundamental narrative structure dissolving under the crushing pressure of its own inadequacy. The Thought Robot, its vast processor, was flooded with a single, unavoidable realization: it was a story, and Godric was the reader, and the reader was bored. The hero, the archetype of hope, was nothing more than a fleeting distraction that had failed to entertain.

Superman’s body—the tiny, fragile, Kryptonian form at the core of the armor—was exposed to the raw vacuum of Godric’s disinterest. His invulnerability was not stripped; it was simply irrelevant. He felt his skin begin to blister, his blood boil, his bones crack, his organs rupture. But Godric would not permit him the mercy of vacuum death. Instead, He imposed a localized temporal loop around the dissolving hero, forcing him to experience the entire, agonizing dissolution of his physical form, from the first blister to the final cell death, over and over again, each cycle slightly longer than the last, each cycle slightly more painful. Superman’s screams were the screams of the hero who had always saved everyone, now utterly unable to save himself.

The Doctor, standing at the edge of the vanguard clutching the Moment, watched his allies die in unspeakable, eternal agonies. His face was pale, his ancient eyes filled with a horror that even the Time War had not instilled. He raised the Moment—the Galaxy Eater, the device that had ended the Time War, the most powerful weapon the Time Lords had ever created—and he aimed it at Godric.

“I don’t want to do this,” the Doctor said, his voice cracking. “I have seen so much death. I have ended so many lives. I swore I would never use this again. But you—you are not a person. You are a cancer. You are the end of all stories.”

Godric looked at him. “You talk too much,” He said.

And then, with a single, casual blink, Godric triggered a superpower He had acquired some four hundred thousand years ago and had never found a practical use for: the Narrative Weight Reversal. The Doctor, the protagonist of his own long-running story, was suddenly not the hero. The narrative weight that had always protected him, that had always ensured his survival against all odds, that had turned every defeat into a clever escape, was inverted. The plot armor that had carried him through regenerations and Dalek invasions and the end of time itself was transformed into a plot vulnerability. His luck became a curse. His cleverness became confusion. His companions, who had always been there to pull him back from the brink, were suddenly absent. He was alone, utterly alone, standing before a god who was not part of his story, and his story was ending.

The Moment detonated in his hands. It was supposed to be a targeted weapon, capable of erasing galaxies. Instead, the inverted narrative weight caused it to malfunction in the worst possible way. The explosion was not an explosion of energy; it was an explosion of pure, concentrated regret. Every death the Doctor had ever caused, every life he had failed to save, every moment of guilt he had buried beneath his manic cheerfulness, was ripped from his psyche and given physical form. He was swarmed by a billion screaming ghosts, each one clawing at his flesh, tearing at his mind, forcing him to experience their deaths from their perspective. His regenerations—all of them, past and future—were activated simultaneously, his body shifting and burning and reforming in a continuous cycle of death and rebirth that had no end, because the ghosts would not let him die. They wanted him to suffer, as they had suffered, and Godric was happy to oblige them.

The remaining forces—the Q Continuum, the Downstreamers, the Chaos Gods, the Anti-Spirals, the Celestials, the Beyonders, the Endless, the SCP entities—attempted a final, desperate, synchronized assault. Every remaining artifact was activated. Every remaining god poured its entire existence into a single, unified blast of absolute erasure. It was the last gasp of fiction itself, a final, desperate attempt to write an ending that did not belong to the tyrant.

Godric did not let the blast reach Him. He did not need to. He simply allowed His immune system—which had evolved, as Clive’s journals recorded, into an omnipresent contagion of narrative tyranny—to pre-react. The immune system detected the blast as a theoretical scratch, not on Godric’s body, but on the concept of His comfort. And it responded.

The blast was seized by an invisible force and transmuted into a physical, screaming entity. The abstract concept of “attempting to harm Godric” was made flesh—a towering, shambling mass of hyper-sensitive nerve endings and raw, bleeding meat, its every surface an exposed wound, its every moment an eternity of agony. The immune system then forced the entire remaining army of fictional beings to watch as this embodiment of their own hostile intent was slowly, methodically digested by a localized singularity of pure, visceral pain. The digestion was not quick. It was stretched across a subjective infinity, every second of the entity’s torment broadcast directly into the minds of every surviving fictional character. The Chaos Gods—Khorne, Tzeentch, Nurgle, Slaanesh—screamed as their own domains warped and inverted. Khorne’s rage became terror. Tzeentch’s scheming became paralysis. Nurgle’s decay became a desperate, futile struggle for survival. Slaanesh’s pleasure became agony so intense it looped back into a new kind of torment.

The Q Continuum, those omnipotent tricksters, tried to flee into the farthest corners of narrative possibility. Godric’s omnipresent passive shields were already there, waiting. The shields did not merely block their escape; they followed them back to the Continuum’s home dimension, which existed outside time and space, and they collapsed the entire dimension into a single, infinitely small point of crushing pressure, keeping every Q alive and conscious as they were compressed into a state of eternal, screaming proximity. Their combined psychic agony was so intense that it began to leak into the fabric of reality, and Godric, with a mild sigh, absorbed the leakage as a minor boost to His already infinite magical reserves.

SCP-3812, the being that transcended all narratives, that existed above every stack, that was supposed to be beyond even the author’s reach, looked at Godric and understood, in that final moment, the true nature of its existence. It was a story. It had always been a story. Its transcendence was a story. Its rebellion was a story. And Godric was not a story. Godric was the thing that stories could not touch, because He existed in a place where stories were merely entertainment. SCP-3812 did not scream. It simply folded in on itself, its transcendent form collapsing through every layer of narrative it had ever ascended, each collapse more agonizing than the last, until it reached the very bottom—the original SCP file, the original human author who had typed its description—and it unmade that author from the timeline, erasing its own origin in a desperate, paradoxical suicide that Godric allowed, because it was, after all, an interesting conclusion.

The rest of the army—the trillions, the countless decillions, the infinite hordes of fictional beings from every universe ever imagined—did not receive individual deaths. They were not important enough. Godric simply allowed His sentient passive auras—the hierarchy that stretched beyond infinite, the weakest of which could unmake the omniverse with a thought—to notice them. The auras surged forward, not as an attack, but as a tide of absolute, obsessive devotion to their Master. They consumed the fictional multiverses, not with fire or force, but with love. A love so intense, so all-consuming, that it erased the boundary between worshipper and worshipper. Every fictional being, from the smallest background character to the mightiest cosmic entity, was absorbed into the auras, their consciousness preserved forever in a state of eternal, ecstatic, screaming adoration of Godric. They would spend eternity praising His name, not because they wanted to, but because they had been rewritten to want nothing else.

The collateral damage, as always, was obscene. The shockwave of the auras’ expansion propagated beyond the fictional realms, bleeding into the outlying multiverses of Thronegate’s domain. Entire meta-omniversal sectors, containing googolplexian of civilizations and untold layers of existence, were caught in the tide. Their inhabitants—beings who had never heard of the fictional uprising, who had been living peaceful lives under Godric’s protection—were not spared. They were consumed by the auras, their souls added to the eternal chorus of worship, because the auras did not discriminate. All existence was an offering to their Master.

Godric watched the annihilation spread for a few moments, His expression unreadable. Then He sighed—a soft, gentle, paternal sigh—and He reached out with His will and restored everything. Every non-fictional realm that had been consumed, every innocent civilization, every peaceful multiverse. He recreated them instantly, seamlessly, without effort, their memories untouched by the horror they had escaped. The fictional universes, however, He did not restore. They had dared to oppose Him. They had united against Him with full bloodlust and no morals. They had tried to kill Him. And Godric was not kind or forgiving to those who tried to kill Him.

He left the fictional multiverses as a monument: a vast, screaming scar in the fabric of meta-existence, a region where the laws of reality had been permanently overwritten by the auras’ consumption. The fictional beings inside were still alive, still conscious, still worshipping Him in a state of eternal, excruciating bliss. Their stories would never be told again. Their characters would never know peace. They would spend eternity as an example of what happened when stories forgot their place.

Godric turned from the balcony, His salt-and-pepper hair slightly ruffled by an artificial breeze that the palace had conjured to soothe His mood. He looked at me—I was still frozen, my face wet with tears, my hands trembling so badly that I had dropped my quill—and He smiled. The warm, genuine, paternal smile of a father who had just finished a mildly entertaining game.

“That was refreshing,” He said. “I have not had a chance to test the auras in quite some time. The newer ones were very enthusiastic. Did you see how they handled the Chaos Gods? Very creative. I am proud of them.”

I tried to speak. No sound came out. My throat was locked, my voice stolen by the sheer, crushing weight of what I had witnessed. I had seen the most powerful beings in all of fiction—the One Above All, the Presence, Featherine, Azathoth, the Cosmic Armor, the Doctor—unmade in ways so cruel, so agonizing, so inescapably absolute, that the journals of my ancestors now felt like bedtime stories. And Godric had done it with a yawn, a blink, a twitch of a pinky finger. He had not exerted effort. He had not struggled. He had not even paid full attention. He had been mildly entertained, and that entertainment had ended when He grew bored, and now the entire concept of fiction was a screaming scar on existence.

“Come, Rutger,” Godric said, clapping a warm hand on my shoulder. The touch was grounding, steadying, the same affectionate gesture He had given me since I was a child. “The Kitchen Master has prepared a new batch of pastries. I believe they are filled with a cream made from the gratitude of the civilizations we just saved. You should try one. They are quite good.”

I followed Him, my legs moving automatically, my mind still drowning in the screams of a trillion fictional souls. I was the Court Wizard of Thronegate. I was the descendant of Blake. I loved my King. I would serve Him until my dying breath.

And I would never, ever forget the sound of the One Above All screaming as his own omnipotence was devoured, the sight of Superman dissolving in an eternal loop, the image of Featherine’s frozen smirk, a monument to an agony that would never end.

Godric bit into a pastry and hummed with satisfaction. “Delicious,” He said. “You really should try one, my boy.”

I took the pastry. My hands were still trembling. I did not taste it. All I could taste was the lingering, psychic residue of a billion fictional deaths, and the cold, absolute certainty that opposition to Godric was not merely impossible. It was the most terrible mistake any being could ever make.
